vitals.date_specific: Last held May 24, 2026; next edition TBD

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 71. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 67.

course_description.character: A 163.4 km, 3,973 m mountain loop through the Vercors massif (crossing briefly into neighboring Drôme near Font d'Urle), starting and finishing at Le Balcon de Villard/Côte 2000 a few kilometers above Villard-de-Lans. The route runs through the Gorges de la Bourne on cliff-cut roads and rock tunnels before climbing to Font d'Urle (the course high point, near 1,500 m) and Col d'Herbouilly, then closes with a short final climb back to Côte 2000. In 2026, most of the route remained open to ordinary traffic outside specified closure zones, so riders shared the mountain with normal local traffic on parts of the course. GFNY also runs a shorter, non-competitive medium course (69.3 km / 1,782 m) alongside the long race — it gets a finish time but alphabetized, unranked results.
course_description.signature_challenge: Col d'Herbouilly's roughly 10 km at 6% arrives after 140+ km already in the legs, following Col de la Croix Perrin and Font d'Urle — the third and hardest of the course's rated climbs, with the Côte 2000 finishing kicker still to come.
suffering_zones: Col de la Croix Perrin: ~4.2 km at 4.7% (pitches to 13%) around km 15, summiting at 1,218 m early in the race.
Font d'Urle: ~6 km at 5.5% beginning near km 79, the course's high point at roughly 1,500 m.
Col d'Herbouilly: ~10 km at 6% cresting around km 144, the longest sustained climb on the course.
Côte 2000: the final classified climb, ~3 km at 5%, arriving after roughly 155 km of accumulated fatigue.

history.origin_story: Debuted in 2022 as a GFNY European Championship race with roughly 250 competitors (per Le Dauphine's inaugural-edition report); the 2026 edition was the fifth and drew over 1,000 riders.
notable_moments: 2020: Lennard Kämna won the Tour de France stage finishing at Côte 2000 after a solo attack, giving the finish climb real pro pedigree ahead of GFNY's 2022 launch.

biased_opinion.verdict: A Real Mountain Race, Undersold By Its Own Data
biased_opinion.summary: GFNY Villard de Lans is a legitimate, growing race through the Vercors that debuted in 2022 as a GFNY European Championship event — 163.4 km and 3,973 m on the competitive long course, with 2026's edition run mostly on open Isère/Drôme public roads outside specified closures around the start, the Gorges de la Bourne and the Herbouilly climb. Field size has grown fast: from roughly 250 riders at the 2022 launch to over 1,000 in 2026, its fifth edition. But the version of this race that was on file here — 96.6 km, 1,707 m — described neither of GFNY's two published routes; it understated the actual long course by nearly 67 km and 2,266 vertical meters, which would have badly misrepresented what riders are signing up for. As with other GFNY events, the medium course (69.3 km / 1,782 m) gets a finish time but only alphabetized, unranked results — only the long course is genuinely competitive.
strengths: Real Vercors mountain terrain: Col de la Croix Perrin, Font d'Urle, Col d'Herbouilly, and the Gorges de la Bourne's cliff-cut roads and rock tunnels
Fast-growing field — from ~250 riders at the 2022 launch to over 1,000 in the 2026 fifth edition
Tour de France pedigree at the finish: Lennard Kämna won the 2020 stage that ended at Côte 2000
Genuine competitive format on the long course — chip timing, age-category classification, and a podium
weaknesses: In 2026, most of the course ran on public roads open to ordinary traffic outside specified closure zones — riders shared the mountain with normal local traffic for long stretches
The medium course (69.3 km/1,782 m) receives a finish time but non-competitive, alphabetized-only results — don't expect a ranked outcome on that distance
Only five editions deep as of 2026, a short track record next to Europe's longer-running Alpine gran fondos
The current-year GFNY jersey is mandatory and entries are effectively locked in once purchased
biased_opinion.bottom_line: A genuine, growing mountain gran fondo in a scenic corner of the Vercors — race the long course for the real competitive format and the full 3,973 m of climbing; treat the medium course as a supported ride, not a race, regardless of how it's marketed.

final_verdict.one_liner: Vercors mountain showcase, growing fast — race the long course, not the undersold profile that used to be on file.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes for the long course if you want a genuine, growing GFNY mountain race through the Vercors with real climbing. Treat the medium course as a supported ride with a finish time, not a ranked race.
final_verdict.alternatives: For a comparable GFNY-format mountain race in France, see GFNY Grand Ballon (Alsace/Vosges). For Alpine climbing with a longer competitive track record, look at the region's established cyclosportives.

rider_intel.search_text: The available transcript for GFNY Villard de Lans contains only music lyrics and audio without any race-specific content or rider commentary about the course, conditions, or racing experience. No actionable intelligence about this road gran fondo in Villard-de-Lans, France could be extracted from the provided material. (Corrected 2026-07-24: this is a paved-road GFNY event, not a gravel race — the prior wording was a leftover Gravel God migration artifact.)

eligibility.notes: Fifth edition ran 24 May 2026 with over 1,000 riders; curl-verified 200 (2026-07-24). GFNY's race finder still lists Villard-de-Lans but without a confirmed future date; a third-party calendar (milesrepublic) tentatively shows May 2027, unconfirmed by the organizer as of this writing. Corrected vitals this wave: distance_km 96.6->163.4, elevation_m 1707->3973 (the on-file numbers matched neither GFNY's long nor medium course and understated the competitive race by ~67km/2,266m); date changed from '2026: May TBD' to '2027: TBD' since the 2026 edition already ran; history.founded corrected 2019->2022 (actual GFNY launch year, per the original announcement and Le Dauphine's inaugural-edition coverage). Via codex gpt-5.6-sol foreground research, editorial wave 5.
